steps.title: Identify the grammatical structures on each side
steps.content:
Left of the blank: a completed quotation expressing the Nobel Prize criterion — “produced the most outstanding work in an idealistic direction” — which forms an independent clause. Right of the blank: “judges recognized Lagerlöf [for] the lofty idealism, vivid imagination and spiritual perception that characterize her writings” — another independent clause, even with its introductory time phrase “in 1909, for instance.”

The blank requires punctuation after the closing quotation mark that properly connects these two complete thoughts.

steps.title: Apply the semicolon rule
steps.content: A semicolon joins two independent clauses when they are closely related. These two clauses are: the first states the Nobel criterion (authors must produce idealistic work); the second provides a concrete historical example of judges recognizing that criterion. Choices A and D introduce grammatically incorrect or inappropriate punctuation after a closing quotation mark; choice C leaves no punctuation between two related independent clauses, leaving them improperly separated. The semicolon properly shows that the example supports and illustrates the criterion. That matches choice B — B is correct.
